name: testing-strategy
title: Define the testing strategy
description: Decide the test mix — unit vs integration vs e2e — what tools to use, what gets gated in CI, and what the coverage expectation is.

starter.summary: Decide what tests run, where, and what blocks merges.
starter.issue: Implicit testing strategies tend toward 'we'll add tests later.' Decide the floor (what's required for green CI) and the ceiling (when do we stop adding tests) explicitly.
assumptions: Language is chosen; default test runner ecosystem is known.
Team has opinions on TDD vs test-after.

positions.title: Unit-heavy, sparse integration, minimal e2e
positions.description: Most tests are pure unit. A handful of integration tests for critical paths. One or two smoke e2es.
cons: False confidence if business logic spans modules

positions.title: Integration-heavy with real dependencies
positions.description: Tests hit real DB / real HTTP. Few mocks. Unit tests only where helpful.

positions.title: E2E-led (Playwright/Cypress as primary gate)
positions.description: E2E tests are the source of truth. Other tests supplement.

positions.title: Property-based + unit + smoke e2e
positions.description: fast-check / Hypothesis for invariants, plus targeted unit & a smoke e2e.
pros: Catches edge cases automated example-based tests miss

implications: CI gating: which tests block merge?
Local dev: which subset runs on save?
Coverage tooling and expected threshold (or no threshold).
Flake policy: what happens when a test goes flaky?

notes_for_agent: For most projects, unit-heavy with sparse integration is the right default. Push back if the project is a transactional system (money, state machines) — those want integration-heavy. Ask the team for their testing pain history.
